title: Short Squeeze Candidates March 2026: Stocks Most Likely t...
description: Discover which stocks have the highest short interest in March 2026. Is a short squeeze brewing? We analyze the data, catalysts, and historical examples.

mainEntity.name: What are the highest short interest stocks in March 2026?

acceptedAnswer.text: As of March 2026, some of the stocks with the highest short interest relative to their float include NVAX (Novavax), LCID (Lucid Group), RUN (Sunrun), and BYND (Beyond Meat). Short interest percentages can fluctuate rapidly, so it is important to check real-time data.

mainEntity.name: What makes a stock a good short squeeze candidate?

acceptedAnswer.text: A good short squeeze candidate typically has a high short percentage of its float (usually >20%), a high 'days to cover' ratio (indicating it would take days of average volume to buy back all shorted shares), a relatively low public float, and a potential catalyst that could drive the price up unexpectedly.

mainEntity.name: How does a short squeeze happen?

acceptedAnswer.text: A short squeeze occurs when a heavily shorted stock rapidly increases in price. Short sellers, who borrowed the stock hoping it would drop, are forced to buy shares back at a higher price to cover their positions and limit losses. This sudden influx of buying pressure drives the price even higher, creating a feedback loop.

Days to cover (also known as the short ratio) is calculated by dividing the total number of shares shorted by the average daily trading volume. It represents the theoretical number of days it would take for all short sellers to cover their positions if volume remained constant.

mainEntity.name: Are high short interest stocks guaranteed to squeeze?

acceptedAnswer.text: No. In fact, most highly shorted stocks are shorted for a reason—the underlying companies may be facing severe financial difficulties. Trading short squeeze candidates is highly speculative and risky, as the price can continue to decline significantly if no positive catalyst occurs.
